meta.title: Mechanics of Materials: Stress, Strain, and Deformation
meta.description: An introductory course to Mechanics of Materials (Strength of Materials). Learn about axial loading, normal stress, shear stress, and Hooke's Law.

hero.h1: Mechanics of Materials: Stress, Strain, and Deformation
hero.intro:
While Statics deals with external forces, Mechanics of Materials looks inside. How do materials respond to those forces? Do they stretch, bend, or break? This course defines the internal resistance of solid bodies to external loads.

Once reactions are known from statics, you must ask whether the material survives. Normal stress σ = F/A and Hooke's law ε = σ/E link force to deformation. Stress concentrations at holes and fillets can locally exceed nominal values.

Every strength calculator in AluCalc — from fasteners to beams — builds on this σ–ε foundation.

stepByStep: Calculate the internal force at the point of interest.
Determine the cross-sectional area of the element.
Apply the stress formula: σ = F / A.
Calculate strain using Hooke's Law: ε = σ / E.
Verify that stress is below the material's yield strength.
Apply stress concentration factor Kt when geometry has discontinuities.
Compare computed stress to yield with an appropriate factor of safety.
whyThisMatters: Prevents structural failure by ensuring material limits are never exceeded.
Allows for the selection of appropriate materials based on loading conditions.
commonMistakes: Neglecting the sign of the stress (Tension vs Compression).
Using the wrong area for shear stress (it's the area parallel to the force).
Failing to account for stress concentrations at holes or fillets.
engineeringTip: In engineering design, we usually target a Factor of Safety (FoS) between 1.5 and 3.0.

calculatorCta.label: Analyze fastener stress with our real-time integrity engine

faq.question: What is the difference between stress and pressure?
faq.answer: Pressure is an external force distributed over an external surface, while stress is an internal resistance distributed over an internal cross-section.

faq.question: What is factor of safety?
faq.answer: Ratio of allowable to computed stress — typically 1.5–3.0 depending on consequence of failure and load uncertainty.

faq.question: How is shear stress different?
faq.answer: Shear acts parallel to the area; area is the section in the plane of shear, not perpendicular to force.

faq.question: When does Hooke's law fail?
faq.answer: Beyond proportional limit / yield — plastic deformation requires different constitutive models.

learningObjectives: Compute normal stress σ = F/A and interpret tension vs compression
Apply Hooke's law to relate stress, strain, and elastic modulus
Identify stress concentrations at holes, fillets, and sudden section changes
keyTakeaways: Stress is internal resistance; pressure acts on external surfaces
Stay below yield with an appropriate factor of safety (1.5–3.0)
Shear area lies in the plane of the shear force — not the normal area
